fullDescription.text: User-controlled input is passed directly to eval(), allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary server-side JavaScript code.

shortDescription.text: NoSQL Injection via unsanitized MongoDB query

fullDescription.text: User-supplied values are inserted into MongoDB queries without sanitization, potentially allowing query manipulation.

shortDescription.text: Insecure Direct Object Reference on userId

fullDescription.text: The userId used in the database update query comes from the session but the update document uses parseInt(userId) for the stored record, while the query filter uses the raw userId. Mismatch between query filter and stored value types can lead to authorization issues.

message.text: Server-Side JavaScript Injection: req.body.preTax is passed directly to eval(). An attacker can submit arbitrary JavaScript (e.g., 'require("child_process").exec("rm -rf /")') as the preTax field value to achieve Remote Code Execution on the server.
Server-Side JavaScript Injection: req.body.afterTax is passed directly to eval(). An attacker can execute arbitrary server-side code by crafting a malicious afterTax input value.
Server-Side JavaScript Injection: req.body.roth is passed directly to eval(). An attacker can execute arbitrary server-side code by crafting a malicious roth input value.

The contributions-dao.js update() method passes user-controlled values (preTax, afterTax, roth) directly into a MongoDB document without type validation or sanitization at the DAO layer. While the route handler calls eval() (a separate, more severe issue), if eval returns an object instead of a number, it could manipulate the MongoDB document structure. The isNaN check occurs after eval, but crafted payloads can pass isNaN while still being objects with valueOf().

In contributions-dao.js update(), the MongoDB query filter uses the raw userId ({userId}) while the document stores parseInt(userId) as parsedUserId. This type mismatch means the upsert query filter and the stored userId field may differ in type (string vs integer), potentially causing duplicate records or query mismatches that could be exploited to read or modify another user's contributions.
